framework_name: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence (Artificial Intelligence Act)
publisher: European Parliament and Council of the European Union
version: Official Journal L 2024/1689, published 12 July 2024, entered into force 1 August 2024
license: EU legal texts are not subject to copyright (Regulation (EU) 2019/1381, Art. 2; Decision 2011/833/EU)
description: The European Union's comprehensive regulation on artificial intelligence. Establishes a risk-based regulatory framework for AI systems, defining obligations for providers, deployers, importers, distributors, and authorised representatives. 113 articles across 13 chapters plus 13 annexes. First comprehensive AI-specific legislation globally.
scope: 62 elements extracted for STRM: 8 competency elements (articles explicitly defining workforce competency requirements) and 54 obligation elements (legal obligations implying workforce competencies). Covers 21 articles spanning AI literacy (Art. 4), prohibited practices (Art. 5), classification rules (Art. 6), high-risk system requirements (Art. 9-15), provider obligations (Art. 16-17, 20), value chain obligations (Art. 22-27), deployer obligations (Art. 26-27), conformity assessment (Art. 43), registration and transparency (Art. 49-50), GPAI obligations (Art. 53, 55), and post-market monitoring (Art. 72-73).
strm_relevance: The EU AI Act is not a workforce framework — it is a regulation defining legal obligations. STRM-005 maps these obligations to the WIDAI KSA pool to answer: what workforce competencies are required to fulfill each regulatory obligation? This is a fundamentally different mapping than STRM-001 through STRM-004 (competency↔competency). Uses Functional rationale per NIST IR 8477 ('how similar are the results of executing the two concepts'). STS-primary for all elements — the competency_implication field bridges the vocabulary gap between legal prose and competency language.

element_types.competency: Articles that explicitly define workforce competency requirements (e.g., Art. 4 AI literacy, Art. 9(8) relevant expertise). 8 elements.
element_types.obligation: Legal obligations that imply workforce competencies needed for fulfillment (e.g., Art. 9(1) risk management system). 54 elements. Each includes a competency_implication field providing the workforce interpretation used for scoring.

methodology_notes.scoring_method: Cross-Encoder STS as primary for all elements (consistent with STRM-001–004). The competency_implication field bridges the vocabulary gap between legal prose and competency language.
methodology_notes.rationale_type: Functional (per NIST IR 8477 Section 4.3) — the appropriate rationale for obligation→competency mapping.
methodology_notes.methodology_evolution: NLI entailment was initially planned as primary for obligation elements. Diagnostic scoring showed near-zero entailment (mean 0.007) — NLI measures strict logical implication, not semantic relatedness. Revised to STS-primary for all elements. See ADR-019.

notes: Source text extracted from EUR-Lex official publication via browser due to egress restrictions on direct download. Each element preserves the original obligation_text (faithful to the regulation) alongside a competency_implication field (workforce competency interpretation used for scoring). Both fields are preserved in rationale files for full provenance.
